title: Golden syrup flapjacks
subtitle: Oat traybake, chewy and caramel-sweet
excerpt: Four ingredients: oats, butter, golden syrup, and sugar. Melted together and pressed into a tin, baked until golden. The key is cutting them while warm; once cold they are too hard to slice without shattering.
sourceNotes: The British flapjack in its current form (oats, golden syrup, butter, and sugar pressed into a traybake tin) is a twentieth-century standard. Golden syrup, produced from cane sugar refining, was available in British kitchens from the 1880s (Lyle's Golden Syrup launched 1883). Oat-based traybakes of the same type appear in Women's Institute recipe collections and British domestic baking manuals from the 1930s onward.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in a single layer, then stack with paper between. Defrost at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keep in an airtight tin for up to a week. They soften slightly on day two.

content.text: Flapjack texture depends on timing. Underbaked flapjacks are soft and fall apart; overbaked ones are hard all the way through. The sweet spot is when the edges are deep amber and the centre is still a shade pale; the centre continues to set as the tin cools. Cut into bars after 5 minutes while the flapjack is still slightly warm and pliable; trying to cut cold flapjacks causes them to shatter along the oat lines.

Heat the oven to 170°C fan. Line a 20 x 30 cm rectangular tin with baking paper. Melt the butter, golden syrup, and light brown sugar together in a small saucepan over low heat, stirring until the sugar dissolves. Remove from the heat. Tip the oats and salt into a large bowl, pour over the butter mixture, and stir until all the oats are coated.

Tip the oat mixture into the prepared tin. Press down firmly and evenly with the back of a spoon or a flat spatula, packing the oats tightly into the tin. Bake for 18 to 20 minutes, until the edges are deep amber and set. The centre will look slightly pale; this is correct. Remove from the oven and leave in the tin for 5 minutes. While still warm, use a sharp knife to score into 16 bars, pressing firmly through to the base. Leave to cool completely in the tin before lifting out.
